audioscript.text: Section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a woman telephoning a tourist office to ask about free activities.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 1 to 6.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will see that there is an example that has been done for you.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: On this occasion only, the conversation relating to this will be played first.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning, this is Burnham tourist office, Martin speaking.
audioscript.speaker: MARTIN

audioscript.text: Oh, hello.
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: I saw a poster about free things to do in the area, and it said people should phone you for information.
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: I'm coming to Burnham with my husband and two children for a few days on June the 27th, or possibly the 28th, and I'd like some ideas for things to do on the 29th.
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: Yes, of course.
audioscript.speaker: MARTIN

audioscript.text: The date that the woman wants information about is 20 June.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: So the letter C has been circled.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now we shall begin.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You should answer the questions as you listen, because you will not hear the recording a second time.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Listen carefully, and answer questions 1 to 6.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning, this is Burnham tourist office, Martin speaking.
audioscript.speaker: MARTIN

audioscript.text: Oh, hello.
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: I saw a poster about free things to do in the area, and it said people should phone you for information.
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: I'm coming to Burnham with my husband and two children for a few days on June the 27th, or possibly the 28th, and I'd like some ideas for things to do on the 29th.
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: Yes, of course.
audioscript.speaker: MARTIN

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: MARTIN

audioscript.text: Then let's start with a couple of events especially for children.
audioscript.speaker: MARTIN

audioscript.text: The art gallery is holding an event called Family Welcome that day, when there are activities and trails to use throughout the gallery.
audioscript.speaker: MARTIN

audioscript.text: That sounds interesting.
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: What time does it start?
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: The gallery opens at 10, and the Family Welcome event runs from 10:30 until 2:00.
audioscript.speaker: MARTIN

audioscript.text: The gallery stays open until 5.
audioscript.speaker: MARTIN

audioscript.text: And several times during the day, they're going to show a short film that the gallery has produced.
audioscript.speaker: MARTIN

audioscript.text: It demonstrates how ceramics are made, and there'll be equipment and materials for children to have a go themselves.
audioscript.speaker: MARTIN

audioscript.text: Last time they ran the event, there was a film about painting, which went down very well with the children, and they're now working on one about sculpture.
audioscript.speaker: MARTIN

audioscript.text: I like the sound of that.
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: And what other events happen in Burnham?
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: Well, do you all enjoy listening to music?
audioscript.speaker: MARTIN

audioscript.text: Oh, yes.
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: Well, there are several free concerts taking place at different times, 1 or 2 in the morning.
audioscript.speaker: MARTIN

audioscript.text: The majority at lunchtime and a couple in the evening, and they range from pop music to Latin American.
audioscript.speaker: MARTIN

audioscript.text: The Latin American could be fun.
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: What time is that?
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: It's being repeated several times in different places.
audioscript.speaker: MARTIN

audioscript.text: They're performing in the Central Library at 1 o'clock, then at 4 it's in the City Museum, and in the evening at 7.30 there's a longer concert in the theater.
audioscript.speaker: MARTIN

audioscript.text: Right, I'll suggest that to the rest of the family.
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: Something else you might be interested in is the boat race along the river.
audioscript.speaker: MARTIN

audioscript.text: Oh, yes, do tell me about that.
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: The race starts at Offord Marina, to the north of Burnham, and goes as far as Summer Pool.
audioscript.speaker: MARTIN

audioscript.text: The best place to watch it from is Charlesworth Bridge, though that does get rather crowded.
audioscript.speaker: MARTIN

audioscript.text: And who's taking part?
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: Well, local boat clubs, but the standard is very high.
audioscript.speaker: MARTIN

audioscript.text: One of them came first in the west of England regional championship in May this year.
audioscript.speaker: MARTIN

audioscript.text: It was the first time a team from Burnham has won.
audioscript.speaker: MARTIN

audioscript.text: It means that next year, they'll be representing the region in the national championship.
audioscript.speaker: MARTIN

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 7 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 7 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now, I've heard something about Paxton Nature Reserve.
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: It's a good place for spotting unusual birds, isn't it?
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: That's right throughout the year.
audioscript.speaker: MARTIN

audioscript.text: There is a lake there, as well as a river.
audioscript.speaker: MARTIN

audioscript.text: And they provide a very attractive habitat, so it's a good idea to bring binoculars if you have them.
audioscript.speaker: MARTIN

audioscript.text: And just at the moment you can see various flowers that are pretty unusual.
audioscript.speaker: MARTIN

audioscript.text: The soil at Paxton isn't very common.
audioscript.speaker: MARTIN

audioscript.text: They're looking good right now.
audioscript.speaker: MARTIN

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: My husband will be particularly interested in that.
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: And there's going to be a talk and slide show about mushrooms, and you'll be able to go out and pick some afterwards, and study the different varieties.
audioscript.speaker: MARTIN

audioscript.text: Huh, and is it possible for children to swim in the river?
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: Yes, part of it has been fenced off to make it safe for children to swim in.
audioscript.speaker: MARTIN

audioscript.text: It's very shallow, and there's a life guard on duty whenever it's open.
audioscript.speaker: MARTIN

audioscript.text: The lake is too deep, so swimming isn't allowed there.
audioscript.speaker: MARTIN

audioscript.text: OK, we must remember to bring their swimming things, in case we go to Paxton.
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: How long does it take to get there by car from Burnham?
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: Hmm. About 20 minutes, but parking is very limited, so it's usually much easier to go by bus, and it takes about the same time.
audioscript.speaker: MARTIN

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: Well, I'll discuss the options with the rest of the family.
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: Thanks very much for all your help.
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: You're welcome.
audioscript.speaker: MARTIN

audioscript.text: Goodbye.
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: Bye.
audioscript.speaker: MARTIN

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

body.title: Free activities in the Burnham area

items: Paxton is a good place for seeing rare <input> all year round.
This is a particularly good time for seeing certain unusual <input>.
Visitors will be able to learn about <input> and then collect some.
Part of the <input> has been made suitable for swimming.
body.title: Paxton Nature Reserve

test_text.title: Changes in Barford over the last 50 years

audioscript.text: Section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a talk given by a woman called Shona Ferguson about changes in a town called Barford.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 15.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 11 to 15.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First of all, let me thank you all for coming to this public meeting to discuss the future of our town.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Our first speaker is Shona Ferguson from Barford Town Council.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Shona.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Thank you.
audioscript.speaker: SHONA

audioscript.text: First I'll briefly give you some background information, then I'll be asking you for your comments on developments in the town.
audioscript.speaker: SHONA

audioscript.text: Well, as you don't need me to tell you, Barford has changed a great deal in the last 50 years.
audioscript.speaker: SHONA

audioscript.text: These are some of the main changes.
audioscript.speaker: SHONA

audioscript.text: 50 years ago, buses linked virtually every part of the town and the neighboring towns and villages.
audioscript.speaker: SHONA

audioscript.text: Most people use them frequently, but not now, because the bus companies concentrate on just the routes that attract most passengers.
audioscript.speaker: SHONA

audioscript.text: So parts of the town are no longer served by buses.
audioscript.speaker: SHONA

audioscript.text: Even replacing old uncomfortable buses with smart new ones has had little impact on passenger numbers.
audioscript.speaker: SHONA

audioscript.text: It's sometimes said that bus fares are too high.
audioscript.speaker: SHONA

audioscript.text: But in relation to average incomes, fares are not much higher than they were 50 years ago.
audioscript.speaker: SHONA

audioscript.text: Changes in the road network are affecting the town.
audioscript.speaker: SHONA

audioscript.text: The center was recently closed to traffic on a trial basis, making it much safer for pedestrians.
audioscript.speaker: SHONA

audioscript.text: The impact of this is being measured.
audioscript.speaker: SHONA

audioscript.text: The new cycle paths separating bikes from cars in most main roads are being used far more than was expected reducing traffic and improving air quality.
audioscript.speaker: SHONA

audioscript.text: And although the council's attempts to have a bypass constructed have failed, we haven't given up hope of persuading the government to change its mind.
audioscript.speaker: SHONA

audioscript.text: Shopping in the town center has changed over the years.
audioscript.speaker: SHONA

audioscript.text: Many of us can remember when the town was crowded with people going shopping.
audioscript.speaker: SHONA

audioscript.text: Numbers have been falling for several years.
audioscript.speaker: SHONA

audioscript.text: Despite efforts to attract shoppers, for instance by opening new car parks.
audioscript.speaker: SHONA

audioscript.text: Some people combine shopping with visits to the town's restaurants and cafes.
audioscript.speaker: SHONA

audioscript.text: Most shops are small independent stores, which is good, but many people prefer to use supermarkets and department stores in nearby large towns, as there are so few well-known chain stores here.
audioscript.speaker: SHONA

audioscript.text: Turning now to medical facilities, the town is served by family doctors in several medical practices, fewer than 50 years ago, but each catering for far more patients.
audioscript.speaker: SHONA

audioscript.text: Our hospital closed 15 years ago, which means journeys to other towns are unavoidable.
audioscript.speaker: SHONA

audioscript.text: On the other hand, there are more dentists than there used to be.
audioscript.speaker: SHONA

audioscript.text: Employment patterns have changed along with almost everything else.
audioscript.speaker: SHONA

audioscript.text: The number of schools and colleges has increased, making that the main employment sector.
audioscript.speaker: SHONA

audioscript.text: Services such as website design and accountancy have grown in importance, and surprisingly perhaps, manufacturing hasn't seen the decline that has affected it in other parts of the country.
audioscript.speaker: SHONA

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 16 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 16 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now, I'll very quickly outline current plans for some of the town's facilities before asking for your comments.
audioscript.speaker: SHONA

audioscript.text: As you'll know, if you regularly use the car park at the railway station, it's usually full.
audioscript.speaker: SHONA

audioscript.text: The railway company applied for permission to replace it with a multi-storey car park.
audioscript.speaker: SHONA

audioscript.text: But that was refused.
audioscript.speaker: SHONA

audioscript.text: Instead, the company has bought some adjoining land, and this will be used to increase the number of parking spaces.
audioscript.speaker: SHONA

audioscript.text: The grand, the old cinema in the High Street, will close at the end of the year.
audioscript.speaker: SHONA

audioscript.text: And reopen on a different site.
audioscript.speaker: SHONA

audioscript.text: You've probably seen the building under construction.
audioscript.speaker: SHONA

audioscript.text: The plan is to have three screens with fewer seats, rather than just the one large auditorium in the old cinema.
audioscript.speaker: SHONA

audioscript.text: I expect many of you shop in the indoor market. It's become more and more shabby-looking, and because of fears about safety, it was threatened with demolition.
audioscript.speaker: SHONA

audioscript.text: The good news is that it will close for 6 weeks to be made safe and redecorated, and the improved building will open in July.
audioscript.speaker: SHONA

audioscript.text: Lots of people use the library,
audioscript.speaker: SHONA

audioscript.text: including school and college students who go there to study.
audioscript.speaker: SHONA

audioscript.text: The council has managed to secure funding to keep the library open later into the evening, twice a week.
audioscript.speaker: SHONA

audioscript.text: We would like to enlarge the building in the not too distant future, but this is by no means definite.
audioscript.speaker: SHONA

audioscript.text: There's no limit on access to the nature reserve on the edge of town.
audioscript.speaker: SHONA

audioscript.text: And this will continue to be the case.
audioscript.speaker: SHONA

audioscript.text: What will change though, is that the council will no longer be in charge of the area.
audioscript.speaker: SHONA

audioscript.text: Instead, it will become the responsibility of a national body that administers most nature reserves in the country.
audioscript.speaker: SHONA

audioscript.text: OK, now let me ask you, if you have...
audioscript.speaker: SHONA

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.speaker: SHONA

utterances.text: Many of us can remember when the town was crowded with people going shopping.
utterances.speaker: SHONA

utterances.text: Numbers have been falling for several years.
utterances.speaker: SHONA

utterances.text: Despite efforts to attract shoppers, for instance by opening new car parks.
utterances.speaker: SHONA

utterances.text: Some people combine shopping with visits to the town's restaurants and cafes.
utterances.speaker: SHONA

utterances.text: Most shops are small independent stores, which is good, but many people prefer to use supermarkets and department stores in nearby large towns, as there are so few well-known chain stores here.
utterances.speaker: SHONA

utterances.text: Turning now to medical facilities, the town is served by family doctors in several medical practices, fewer than 50 years ago, but each catering for far more patients.
utterances.speaker: SHONA

utterances.text: Our hospital closed 15 years ago, which means journeys to other towns are unavoidable.
utterances.speaker: SHONA

utterances.text: On the other hand, there are more dentists than there used to be.
utterances.speaker: SHONA

utterances.text: Employment patterns have changed along with almost everything else.
utterances.speaker: SHONA

utterances.text: The number of schools and colleges has increased, making that the main employment sector.
utterances.speaker: SHONA

utterances.text: Services such as website design and accountancy have grown in importance, and surprisingly perhaps, manufacturing hasn't seen the decline that has affected it in other parts of the country.
utterances.speaker: SHONA

utterances.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 16 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen and answer questions 16 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now, I'll very quickly outline current plans for some of the town's facilities before asking for your comments.
utterances.speaker: SHONA

utterances.text: As you'll know, if you regularly use the car park at the railway station, it's usually full.
utterances.speaker: SHONA

utterances.text: The railway company applied for permission to replace it with a multi-storey car park.
utterances.speaker: SHONA

utterances.text: But that was refused.
utterances.speaker: SHONA

utterances.text: Instead, the company has bought some adjoining land, and this will be used to increase the number of parking spaces.
utterances.speaker: SHONA

utterances.text: The grand, the old cinema in the High Street, will close at the end of the year.
utterances.speaker: SHONA

utterances.text: And reopen on a different site.
utterances.speaker: SHONA

utterances.text: You've probably seen the building under construction.
utterances.speaker: SHONA

utterances.text: The plan is to have three screens with fewer seats, rather than just the one large auditorium in the old cinema.
utterances.speaker: SHONA

utterances.text: I expect many of you shop in the indoor market. It's become more and more shabby-looking, and because of fears about safety, it was threatened with demolition.
utterances.speaker: SHONA

utterances.text: The good news is that it will close for 6 weeks to be made safe and redecorated, and the improved building will open in July.
utterances.speaker: SHONA

utterances.text: Lots of people use the library,
utterances.speaker: SHONA

utterances.text: including school and college students who go there to study.
utterances.speaker: SHONA

utterances.text: The council has managed to secure funding to keep the library open later into the evening, twice a week.
utterances.speaker: SHONA

utterances.text: We would like to enlarge the building in the not too distant future, but this is by no means definite.
utterances.speaker: SHONA

utterances.text: There's no limit on access to the nature reserve on the edge of town.
utterances.speaker: SHONA

utterances.text: And this will continue to be the case.
utterances.speaker: SHONA

utterances.text: What will change though, is that the council will no longer be in charge of the area.
utterances.speaker: SHONA

utterances.text: Instead, it will become the responsibility of a national body that administers most nature reserves in the country.
utterances.speaker: SHONA

utterances.text: OK, now let me ask you, if you have...
utterances.speaker: SHONA

utterances.text: That is the end of section 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: In Shona's opinion, why do fewer people use buses in Barford these days?
options: The buses are old and uncomfortable.
Fares have gone up too much.
There are not so many bus routes.

items.title: What change in the road network is known to have benefited the town most?
options: the banning of cars from certain streets

items.title: What is the problem affecting shopping in the town centre?
What does Shona say about medical facilities in Barford?
options: The number of dentists is too low.

items.title: The largest number of people are employed in

body.title: Changes in Barford over the last 50 years

text: What is planned for each of the following facilities?
textReadable: What is planned for each of the following facilities?

list: It will move to a new location.
It will have its opening hours extended.
It will be used for a different purpose.
It will have its opening hours reduced.

cinema                   <input>
indoor market            <input>
library                  <input>
nature reserve           <input>
body.title: Facilities

audioscript.text: Section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear two students called Helen and Jeremy, who are studying creative writing, discussing a project for their course, which involves writing and illustrating stories for children.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 21 to 26.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully, and answer questions 21 to 26.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hello, Helen.
audioscript.speaker: JEREMY

audioscript.text: Sorry I'm late.
audioscript.speaker: JEREMY

audioscript.text: Hi, Jeremy.
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: No problem.
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: Well, we'd better work out where we are on our project, I suppose.
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: Yeah, I've looked at the drawings you've done for my story, 'The Forest'.
audioscript.speaker: JEREMY

audioscript.text: And I think they're brilliant.
audioscript.speaker: JEREMY

audioscript.text: They really create the atmosphere I had in mind when I was writing it.
audioscript.speaker: JEREMY

audioscript.text: Uh. I'm glad you like them.
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: There are just a few suggestions I'd like to make.
audioscript.speaker: JEREMY

audioscript.text: Go ahead.
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: Now, I'm not sure about the drawing of the cave.
audioscript.speaker: JEREMY

audioscript.text: It's got trees all around it, which is great, but the drawing's a bit too static, isn't it?
audioscript.speaker: JEREMY

audioscript.text: I think it needs some action.
audioscript.speaker: JEREMY

audioscript.text: Yes, there's nothing happening.
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: Perhaps I should add the boy Malcolm, isn't it?
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: He would be walking up to it.
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: Yes, let's have Malcolm in the drawing.
audioscript.speaker: JEREMY

audioscript.text: And what about putting in a tiger?
audioscript.speaker: JEREMY

audioscript.text: The one that he makes friends with a bit later.
audioscript.speaker: JEREMY

audioscript.text: Maybe it could be sitting under a tree washing itself.
audioscript.speaker: JEREMY

audioscript.text: And the tiger stops in the middle of what it's doing when it sees Malcolm walking past.
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: That's a good idea.
audioscript.speaker: JEREMY

audioscript.text: OK, I'll have a go at that.
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: Then there's the drawing of the crowd of men and women dancing.
audioscript.speaker: JEREMY

audioscript.text: They're just outside the forest, and there's a lot going on.
audioscript.speaker: JEREMY

audioscript.text: That's right.
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: You wanted them to be watching a carnival procession, but I thought it would be too crowded.
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: Do you think it works like this?
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: Yes, I like what you've done.
audioscript.speaker: JEREMY

audioscript.text: The only thing is, could you add Malcolm to it without changing what's already there?
audioscript.speaker: JEREMY

audioscript.text: What about having him sitting on the tree trunk on the right of the picture?
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: Yes, that would be fine.
audioscript.speaker: JEREMY

audioscript.text: And do you want him watching the other people?
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: No, he's been left out of all the fun, so I'd like him to be crying.
audioscript.speaker: JEREMY

audioscript.text: That'll contrast nicely with the next picture.
audioscript.speaker: JEREMY

audioscript.text: Where he's laughing at the clowns in the carnival.
audioscript.speaker: JEREMY

audioscript.text: Right, I'll do that.
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: And then the drawing of the people ice skating in the forest.
audioscript.speaker: JEREMY

audioscript.text: I wasn't too happy with that one.
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: Because they're supposed to be skating on grass, aren't they?
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: That's right, and it's frozen over.
audioscript.speaker: JEREMY

audioscript.text: At the moment, it doesn't look quite right.
audioscript.speaker: JEREMY

audioscript.text: I see what you mean.
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: I'll have another go at that.
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: and I like the wool hats they're wearing.
audioscript.speaker: JEREMY

audioscript.text: Maybe you could give each of them a scarf as well.
audioscript.speaker: JEREMY

audioscript.text: Yeah, that's easy enough.
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: They can be streaming out behind the people to suggest they're skating really fast.
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: great.
audioscript.speaker: JEREMY

audioscript.text: Well, that's all on the drawings.
audioscript.speaker: JEREMY

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: So you've finished writing your story, and I just need to finish illustrating it. And my story and your drawings are done.
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the discussion, you have some time to look at questions 27 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 27 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: So, the next thing is to decide what exactly we need to write about in the report that goes with the stories, and how we're going to divide the work.
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: Right, Helen.
audioscript.speaker: JEREMY

audioscript.text: What do you think about including a section on how we planned the project as a whole, Jeremy?
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: That's probably quite important.
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: Yeah, well you've had most of the good ideas so far.
audioscript.speaker: JEREMY

audioscript.text: How do you feel about drafting something?
audioscript.speaker: JEREMY

audioscript.text: Then we can go through it together and discuss it.
audioscript.speaker: JEREMY

audioscript.text: OK, that seems reasonable.
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: And I could include something on how we came up with the ideas for our two stories, couldn't I?
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: Well, I've started writing something about that, so why don't you do the same, and we can include the two things.
audioscript.speaker: JEREMY

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: So what about our interpretation of the stories?
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: Do we need to write about what we think they show, like the value of helping other people, all that sort of thing?
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: That's gonna come up later, isn't it?
audioscript.speaker: JEREMY

audioscript.text: I think everyone in the class is going to read each other's stories and come up with their own interpretations.
audioscript.speaker: JEREMY

audioscript.text: Which we're going to discuss.
audioscript.speaker: JEREMY

audioscript.text: Oh, I missed that.
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: So it isn't going to be part of the report at all.
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: No, but we need to write about the illustrations because they're an essential element of children's experience of reading the stories.
audioscript.speaker: JEREMY

audioscript.text: It's probably easiest for you to write that section, as you know more about drawing than I do.
audioscript.speaker: JEREMY

audioscript.text: Maybe, but I find it quite hard to write about.
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: I'd be happier if you did it.
audioscript.speaker: HELEN

audioscript.text: OK, so when do you think...
audioscript.speaker: JEREMY

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

body.title: Parts of the report

audioscript.text: Section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear part of a lecture to business students about the use of one particular type of research known as ethnography.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully, and answer questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: So what I'm going to talk about to you today is something called ethnography.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This is a type of research aimed at exploring the way human cultures work.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It was first developed for use in anthropology, and it's also been used in sociology and communication studies.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So, what's it got to do with business?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: You may ask.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Well, businesses are finding that ethnography can offer them deeper insight into the possible needs of customers, either present or future, as well as providing valuable information about their attitudes towards existing products.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And ethnography can also help companies to design new products or services that customers really want.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Let's look at some examples of how ethnographic research works in business.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: One team of researchers did a project for a company manufacturing kitchen equipment.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They watched how cooks used measuring cups to measure out things like sugar and flour.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They saw that the cooks had to check and recheck the contents.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Because although the measuring cups had numbers inside them, the cooks couldn't see these easily.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So, a new design of cup was developed to overcome this problem.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And it was a top seller.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Another team of ethnographic researchers looked at how cell phones were used in Uganda in Africa.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They found that people who didn't have their own phones could pay to use the phones of local entrepreneurs, because these customers paid in advance for their calls.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They were eager to know how much time they'd spent on the call so far.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So the phone company designed phones for use globally with this added feature.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Ethnographic research has also been carried out in computer companies.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In one company, IT systems administrators were observed for several weeks.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It was found that a large amount of their work involved communicating with colleagues in order to solve problems, but that they didn't have a standard way of exchanging information from spreadsheets and so on.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So, the team came up with an idea for software that would help them to do this.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In another piece of research, a team observed and talked to nurses working in hospitals.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This led to the recognition that the nurses needed to access the computer records of their patients, no matter where they were.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This led to the development of a portable computer tablet that allowed the nurses to check records in locations throughout the hospital.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Occasionally, research can be done even in environments where the researchers can't be present.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: For example, in one project done for an airline.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Respondents used their smartphones to record information during airline trips, in a study aiming at tracking the emotions of passengers during a flight.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So, what makes studies like these different from ordinary research?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Let's look at some of the general principles behind ethnographic research in business.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: First of all, the researcher has to be completely open-minded.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: He or she hasn't thought up a hypothesis to be tested, as is the case in other types of research.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Instead, they wait for the participants in the research to inform them.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As far as choosing the participants themselves is concerned, that's not really all that different from ordinary research.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The criteria according to which the participants are chosen may be something as simple as the age bracket they fall into, or the researchers may select them according to their income.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Or they might try to find a set of people who all use a particular product for example, but it's absolutely crucial to recruit the right people as participants.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As well as the criteria I've mentioned, they have to be comfortable talking about themselves, and being watched as they go about their activities.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Actually, most researchers say that people open up pretty easily.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Maybe because they're often in their own home or workplace.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So, what makes this type of research special is that it's not just a matter of sending a questionnaire to the participants.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Instead, the research is usually based on first-hand observation of what they are doing at the time.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But that doesn't mean that the researcher never talks to the participants.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: However, unlike in traditional research, in this case, it's the participant rather than the researchers who decides what direction the interview will follow.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This means that there's less likelihood of the researcher imposing his or her own ideas on the participant.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But after they've said goodbye to their participants and got back to their office, the researcher's work isn't finished.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Most researchers estimate that 70 to 80% of their time is spent not on the collecting of data, but on its analysis, looking at photos, listening to recordings and transcribing them, and so on.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The researchers may end up with hundreds of pages of notes, and to determine what's significant, they don't focus on the sensational things or the unusual things.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Instead, they try to identify a pattern of some sort in all this data, and to discern the meaning behind it.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This can result in some compelling insights.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That can in turn feed back to the whole design process.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of Section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Section 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You will hear part of a lecture to business students about the use of one particular type of research known as ethnography.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen carefully, and answer questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: So what I'm going to talk about to you today is something called ethnography.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This is a type of research aimed at exploring the way human cultures work.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It was first developed for use in anthropology, and it's also been used in sociology and communication studies.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So, what's it got to do with business?
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: You may ask.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Well, businesses are finding that ethnography can offer them deeper insight into the possible needs of customers, either present or future, as well as providing valuable information about their attitudes towards existing products.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And ethnography can also help companies to design new products or services that customers really want.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Let's look at some examples of how ethnographic research works in business.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: One team of researchers did a project for a company manufacturing kitchen equipment.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: They watched how cooks used measuring cups to measure out things like sugar and flour.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: They saw that the cooks had to check and recheck the contents.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Because although the measuring cups had numbers inside them, the cooks couldn't see these easily.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So, a new design of cup was developed to overcome this problem.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And it was a top seller.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Another team of ethnographic researchers looked at how cell phones were used in Uganda in Africa.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: They found that people who didn't have their own phones could pay to use the phones of local entrepreneurs, because these customers paid in advance for their calls.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: They were eager to know how much time they'd spent on the call so far.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So the phone company designed phones for use globally with this added feature.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Ethnographic research has also been carried out in computer companies.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In one company, IT systems administrators were observed for several weeks.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It was found that a large amount of their work involved communicating with colleagues in order to solve problems, but that they didn't have a standard way of exchanging information from spreadsheets and so on.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So, the team came up with an idea for software that would help them to do this.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In another piece of research, a team observed and talked to nurses working in hospitals.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This led to the recognition that the nurses needed to access the computer records of their patients, no matter where they were.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This led to the development of a portable computer tablet that allowed the nurses to check records in locations throughout the hospital.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Occasionally, research can be done even in environments where the researchers can't be present.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: For example, in one project done for an airline.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Respondents used their smartphones to record information during airline trips, in a study aiming at tracking the emotions of passengers during a flight.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So, what makes studies like these different from ordinary research?
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Let's look at some of the general principles behind ethnographic research in business.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: First of all, the researcher has to be completely open-minded.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: He or she hasn't thought up a hypothesis to be tested, as is the case in other types of research.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Instead, they wait for the participants in the research to inform them.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: As far as choosing the participants themselves is concerned, that's not really all that different from ordinary research.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The criteria according to which the participants are chosen may be something as simple as the age bracket they fall into, or the researchers may select them according to their income.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Or they might try to find a set of people who all use a particular product for example, but it's absolutely crucial to recruit the right people as participants.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: As well as the criteria I've mentioned, they have to be comfortable talking about themselves, and being watched as they go about their activities.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Actually, most researchers say that people open up pretty easily.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Maybe because they're often in their own home or workplace.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So, what makes this type of research special is that it's not just a matter of sending a questionnaire to the participants.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Instead, the research is usually based on first-hand observation of what they are doing at the time.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But that doesn't mean that the researcher never talks to the participants.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: However, unlike in traditional research, in this case, it's the participant rather than the researchers who decides what direction the interview will follow.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This means that there's less likelihood of the researcher imposing his or her own ideas on the participant.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But after they've said goodbye to their participants and got back to their office, the researcher's work isn't finished.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Most researchers estimate that 70 to 80% of their time is spent not on the collecting of data, but on its analysis, looking at photos, listening to recordings and transcribing them, and so on.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The researchers may end up with hundreds of pages of notes, and to determine what's significant, they don't focus on the sensational things or the unusual things.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Instead, they try to identify a pattern of some sort in all this data, and to discern the meaning behind it.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This can result in some compelling insights.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That can in turn feed back to the whole design process.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of Section 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER
